summary: McKenzie describes his initial foray into email marketing for Bingo Card Creator's web application. He chose MailChimp for its API and integrated it using the hominid Ruby gem in about 4 hours. His strategy includes monthly newsletters timed to seasonal demand peaks and a two-email onboarding sequence for new signups. He sets a clear profitability threshold of one sale per thousand emails at 2 cents each.
key_points: Email marketing enables re-engagement of seasonal users who signed up months earlier, turning one-time visitors into repeat customers
A two-email drip sequence after signup serves dual purpose: onboarding education and purchase nudging
At 2 cents per email, the break-even point is roughly 1 sale per 1,000 emails sent, making the economics straightforward to evaluate
Integration with MailChimp via API took only 4 hours, demonstrating low barrier to entry for email marketing
money_quotes: Since an absolutely ginormous portion of my business is seasonal, this will let me get in touch with the thousands of users who will sign up before Halloween again at Halloween, and remind them that they can have a great activity ready for only $30.
The emails run me 2 cents apiece, so I'll be looking to see if they generate enough marginal business (about 1 sale per every thousand) to justify.
The first covers basic how-to-use instructions and is just to jog people's memory that they signed up.
The 2nd is to show some of the more advanced features of BCC and it will probably include a strong suggestion that they buy it… maybe a coupon, too.
key_insight: Email marketing turns a free web app's user registrations into a remarketing channel, letting you re-engage seasonal users at minimal cost with a clear, measurable ROI threshold.
